mappings.rationaleAI: The 1910-1920 revolution, the 1917 constitution's land reform and labor rights provisions, and the factional struggles among Zapata, Villa, and the constitutionalists directly produced the post-revolutionary order described in A.1 (Obregon's coup, Calles' National Revolutionary Party) and A.2 (Cardenas' social reforms and petroleum expropriation built on the constitutional framework).

U.S. Cold War interventionism, economic imperialism, and support for authoritarian regimes are central themes across the section, from the CIA-sponsored coup in Guatemala (B.1.a.ii) and Contra insurgency in Nicaragua (B.1.d) to the Bay of Pigs and missile crisis with Cuba (B.2.c.iv), military interventions in the Dominican Republic (B.2.b), Haiti (B.2.a), Panama (B.1.f), and Grenada (B.2.d), and trade relations with Mexico (A.4.b).

Kahlo's life and art are inseparable from post-revolutionary Mexico (1920s-1950s), the cultural nationalism of the Obregon/Calles era (A.1), and the Cardenas period (A.2) when she and Rivera were central figures in Mexican cultural politics.
Chile under Pinochet and the politics of memory connect to Chilean politics since 1952 including the Pinochet military regime (E.3.b).
